title: Devilled chicken livers
subtitle: Chicken livers in a spiced butter sauce, on toast
excerpt: Chicken livers seared quickly in butter with mustard, Worcestershire sauce, and cayenne, then spooned onto thick toast. Anglo-Indian devilling at its fastest — a starter that takes ten minutes and eats like something much more considered.
sourceNotes: Based on the Anglo-Indian devilled tradition documented in Lizzie Collingham, 'Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors' (2005) and Colonel A.R. Kenney-Herbert (Wyvern), 'Culinary Jottings for Madras' (1878, public domain). Devilling (coating food in a pungent paste of condiments before cooking) was one of the defining techniques of the Anglo-Indian table and appeared in virtually every household cookbook of the colonial period.

content.text: Devilling was the Anglo-Indian technique of making food pungent: dry mustard, cayenne, and Worcestershire sauce applied to meat or offal before a quick blast of heat. The livers here are seared hard for 2–3 minutes so they colour on the outside and remain pink in the centre — longer and they become grainy and bitter. The butter and the condiments make a small, sharp sauce in the pan that soaks into the toast as it sits.

The quality of the livers matters. Fresh chicken livers should smell clean and look uniformly dark red-brown, with no grey areas. Trim the membrane and any green or discoloured patches before cooking — the green comes from bile and is bitter.

items.prepNote: trimmed of membrane and any green patches

content.text: Pat {{chicken-liver}} of trimmed chicken livers dry with kitchen paper. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{cayenne-pepper}} of cayenne. Heat a cast-iron pan over a high heat until very hot. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter. When it foams and begins to colour, add the livers in a single layer — do not crowd the pan. Sear for 2 minutes without moving, then turn and sear for 1 more minute. The livers should be browned outside and still slightly pink inside.
Add the devilling sauce and serve

Reduce the heat to medium. Add {{garlic}} crushed garlic to the pan and stir for 30 seconds. Add {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce and {{dijon-mustard}} of mustard. Stir to coat the livers and let the sauce bubble for 30 seconds. Spoon immediately over thick toast. Scatter {{parsley}} of chopped parsley over and serve with {{lemon}} lemon wedges.

items.symptom: Livers are grey and grainy throughout
items.fix: Chicken livers cook in 3 minutes total. They should still be pink at the centre when you spoon them onto the toast. They continue cooking from residual heat.

items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or livers not dried before cooking
items.fix: Pat completely dry and use the hottest pan possible. Steam from wet livers prevents browning.

items.cause: Green bile patches not fully trimmed
items.fix: Cut away every green or discoloured patch before cooking. Even a small amount of bile will make the whole batch taste bitter.

content.text: Devilling appears throughout Anglo-Indian cooking as a technique for breakfast dishes, snacks, and starters (devilled kidneys, devilled bones, devilled sardines), united by the combination of dry mustard, Worcestershire sauce, and cayenne. Colonel Kenney-Herbert, whose 'Culinary Jottings for Madras' (1878) is one of the foundational texts of Anglo-Indian domestic cooking, includes several devilled preparations and credits them as a defining characteristic of British household cooking in India. The technique was practical — condiments kept longer than spice pastes in a hot climate — and the results were reliably sharp and immediate.
